title: What Happened That Day? What?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored, the corrupted 1991 Governor’s Address date has been verified from the printed page as `ஜூலை 4`, and clear OCR symbols and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Holding the office of Chief Minister demands a certain dignity. If people who ask which shop sells such dignity are seated in that office, they can only display their true nature. Panneerselvam, who has become the iluppai blossom in a town without a sugar mill, claimed in yesterday's statement that the DMK had indulged in disorder in the Assembly. The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam has been represented in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly since 1957. It entered with fifteen members, grew to fifty, won a majority in 1967 and formed the Government under Perarignar Anna. The Congress, which then governed all India, sat in opposition in the Tamil Nadu Assembly. But the people of Tamil Nadu and journalists will surely not have forgotten that disorder and indecency entered the House only after an organisation called the AIADMK entered it, especially after the death of my dear friend M.G.R.

More particularly, even before a conflict arose between the DMK and the AIADMK, the latter split into the AIADMK (Ja) and AIADMK (Je) factions. On 28 January 1988, when M.G.R.'s wife Janaki Ammal was Chief Minister, members of the rival Jayalalithaa faction started a disturbance, turned the Assembly into a battlefield and allowed matters to spin so far out of control that police led by DGP Devaram entered the House and attacked members. Just as Jayalalithaa expected, Janaki Ammal's Government was then dismissed on the strength of that incident.

Have people forgotten how AIADMK members struck one another as though fighting in a marketplace? Do they not know that microphones provided for speeches were ripped out and used as weapons? Photographs can still be found in old newspapers of AIADMK MLAs emerging in a victory march, microphones in hand like warriors carrying swords on a battlefield! Does Panneerselvam not know of this incident in which every trace of the House's civility and culture was smashed?

Searching high and low, Panneerselvam has found 25 March 1989 and invented a tale that the DMK indulged in disorder when, as Chief Minister and Finance Minister, I tried to present new schemes in a Budget after thirteen years. Has he forgotten that the whole event was staged like a play, that its truth was exposed long ago, and that the people expressed their anguish? Since he himself has written about it, let us recall what was said in the House about what happened that day, what entered the Assembly record and the later efforts to remove it from that record.

When I presented the Budget in the Assembly on 25 March 1989, Tirunavukkarasar, now a senior Congress leader and then one of Jayalalithaa's closest supporters in her party, gave the members and the country a detailed explanation. Rather than offer my own account of the violence that occurred while I read the Budget, I reproduce exactly what Tirunavukkarasu, then the Deputy Leader of the Opposition and a Jayalalithaa supporter in the AIADMK, said in the same Assembly a year or two later. Here is our friend Tirunavukkarasu speaking:

“Everyone knows that an untoward incident occurred in this very Assembly when the Honourable Chief Minister Kalaignar presented the Budget. What I now say, I say upon my mother and swear upon M.G.R., whom I worship as God.

“When the first Budget session was to be held, Selvi Jayalalithaa, then Leader of the Opposition, seated all the AIADMK legislators in her house and told me: ‘I am the principal Leader of the Opposition. It would not look proper if I myself grabbed and pulled at the Chief Minister while he reads the Budget. So those of you beside me must snatch the Budget copy from Chief Minister Karunanidhi's hand and hit him.’

“I immediately answered in great distress: ‘Whether knowingly or unknowingly, Revolutionary Leader M.G.R. has left us after keeping us in dignified positions for ten years. Please do not turn us into hired muscle. My conscience will not permit it. Second, we do not need an incident of this kind.’ I argued further: ‘We may dislike the Chief Minister. That is a different matter. But behind him sit a majority of legislators who have accepted him as their leader. If you lay a hand on him, they will become emotional. There will certainly be blows and a melee. It will become ugly. Newspapers will write shamefully of it. So many problems arose while the Revolutionary Leader was alive. Had anyone even approached him, we would not have stood by; we would have attacked. In the same way, the legislators behind today's Chief Minister will certainly strike back.’

“I asked, ‘Have you consulted all the senior leaders?’ She replied, ‘I have seated them all in the dining hall because they would not agree if I asked them.’ The moment I said, ‘I cannot do it,’ she looked at the legislators and said, ‘Very well, he says he will not. Whichever of you is in a position to do it, go and snatch the Budget as it is being read and tear it up.’ Then she went upstairs.
“Dr Hande, Raghavanandam, Madhavan, S.D.S. and the other senior leaders of the time were sitting in the dining hall. I went there and quarrelled with them, asking, ‘Who gave this advice?’

“Selvi Jayalalithaa came downstairs. As soon as she arrived, she looked at those present and said, ‘Let us leave; it is time.’ None of the senior leaders had the courage, given the speed with which she came, to stop and advise her. After taking her to the Assembly and seating her, I asked, ‘Have you consulted our allied party, the Congress, about this?’ She said, ‘I am telling you this after consulting everyone. I have received information that if violence occurs in the Assembly today, the Government will be dismissed this very evening.’

“I immediately went to the room of Congress leader Moopanar and said, ‘We plan to stage a walkout today. You too must cooperate with us.’ I also asked indirectly, without stating it outright, ‘If any untoward incident occurs today, will you offer some assistance from within the House?’ He replied, ‘Before leaving, I spoke to Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi at 8.30. Our instruction from Delhi is that the Congress should read a statement condemning the police and then walk out.’

“Selvi Jayalalithaa was seated in the House. I hurried back and told her, ‘I asked Mr Moopanar. They are not prepared to cause any untoward incident here. The Congress members are going to walk out.’ She replied, ‘Moopanar is always like this. He will always speak against me. I have received different information.’ She also asked, ‘Why did you go and consult him?’

“Everyone knows all that subsequently occurred in the Assembly that day. Following the violence, blows and a melee broke out inside the House. Afterwards, I and K.K.S.S.R. sat in the front and back of the car and took Selvi Jayalalithaa away. Until we reached home, she kept saying, ‘They are going to dismiss the Government this very evening; they are going to dismiss the Government.’”

Mr Panneerselvam, does even this incident, planned and staged with malicious intent, return to your memory? Tirunavukkarasar had worked sincerely in the AIADMK. After leaving it, he disclosed this so that the truth would not remain hidden from the country.

When I bring these old and true events to light, does it become a Chief Minister to say that both I and younger brother Stalin are issuing statements beneath our dignity? Is asking you to convene the Assembly a low act? Is that how things are under this Government? Were we alone in calling for the Assembly to meet? All the other opposition parties also asked that it be convened. Must every response invoke father and son? Why, if events involving your own younger brother were recounted, the whole town would laugh!

Whom did all the Public Works Department contractors meet during the last Deepavali? What happened? How many days has it been since a weekly magazine published an article about it and its stench spread everywhere? You have neither the capacity nor the means to answer any of that. And you challenge me?

Do you know this? When the new Secretariat was being built at the Omandurar Estate, I visited it every day to inspect the work. I was the one who said that Jayalalithaa, as Leader of the Opposition, must be provided accommodation just like that arranged for the Chief Minister. Not only that: I personally inspected the room provided for the Leader of the Opposition and, finding it somewhat small, directed that it be enlarged. And you speak to me of standards?

When the Tamil Nadu Assembly met on 8 February 2013, an MLA named Tamilazhagan, who had won on the DMDK ticket and was later said to have broken away in defiance of the party leadership, spoke in the House. He openly invited other DMDK members to make the same decision as he had, saying that because he had left the party the Government was doing good for his constituency and that they too could benefit. DMDK legislators objected to this speech. A dispute arose between the two sides. It was an affair among members elected under the DMDK banner. Yet, on the allegation that a member elected for the DMDK but acting outside its control had been attacked, proceedings were initiated against six members who remained steadfast in the DMDK, and the matter was referred to the Privileges Committee.

Within a month, the Privileges Committee investigated and submitted its report. On that basis, the six MLAs were barred from attending the Assembly for a full year. They were also denied their salaries for that year. The DMK, Congress, CPI(M) and Puthiya Tamilagam walked out in protest. This was the fate of a party that had allied with the AIADMK in the previous Assembly election and worked for its victory. Who caused this indecent episode?

On 24 November 1986, under M.G.R.'s Government, the Speaker read a ruling permanently expelling Professor, the DMK leader, and S. Balan, A. Selvarasan, Parithi Ilamvazhuthi, P. Ponnurangam, Coimbatore M. Ramanathan, Dharmapuri R. Chinnasamy, Alandur M. Abraham, Madurantakam C. Arumugam and Arakkonam V.K. Rasu from the Assembly for burning a copy of the Constitution as one stage of the language agitation. Despite repeated appeals by the opposition parties, the ruling AIADMK refused to withdraw that resolution until the very end.

Another example: in the Assembly under the AIADMK Government in 1991, the Governor delivered his Address on 4 July. As he began, Panruti Ramachandran, then a PMK member, started reading a statement in English in the House. An AIADMK member approached and attacked him.

An incident on 22 March 1999! During the debate on the Budget, an AIADMK member punched Minister Veerapandi Arumugam in the face. The ring he wore struck Arumugam's nose and drew blood. The AIADMK member who attacked him later left that party and joined the DMK itself.

Here is another example. On 7 January 2011, during the DMK Government, AIADMK members caused a disturbance while the Governor delivered his Address and violently attacked the Assembly marshals who came to remove them. One member kicked the marshals, snatched their caps and struck them in the face with those very caps. The photographs appeared in newspapers at the time. Speaker R. Avudaiyappan ordered the expulsion of the nine AIADMK members who had transgressed during the Governor's Address, not for months or years but only for the remainder of that session. The AIADMK members walked out in protest. But do you know what happened the next day?

Speaking in the House the following day as Chief Minister, I said: “The ten DMK legislators, including Professor, who were expelled under an AIADMK Government were not allowed back by that Government. But, on behalf of today's DMK Government that follows Anna's path, I request the expelled AIADMK members to return to the House and cooperate.”

Another incident occurred on the morning of 18 October 2007. Local Administration Minister, younger brother M.K. Stalin, was speaking on a breach-of-privilege issue. Every AIADMK member in the House rose as a body, shouted and caused a tremendous commotion, preventing him from speaking and obstructing the proceedings. They left their seats as a group, sat on the floor before the Speaker's chair and behaved without dignity. The Speaker repeatedly urged them to return to their seats, but they refused. He therefore summoned the marshals and ordered their removal.

At that point, A.K. Bose, MLA for Thiruparankundram, snatched the cap of marshal P. Mathivanan, who had come to remove him, and flung it at the Speaker. Every member of the House saw it. Although it was first resolved under the DMK Government that he should be suspended for six months for an act that attacked the Speaker himself, I intervened immediately and said a suspension for only the first ten days of the next session would be enough. That was what was implemented. This is another example from a DMK Government.
Has there ever been another instance in which Leader of the Opposition Vijayakant himself was barred from Assembly proceedings for ten days? That too happened only under an AIADMK Government.

On 13 November 2008, during the DMK Government, I was not present in the House when I learned that the AIADMK, the principal opposition, had been expelled. I thought: “They were keen to participate in the Electricity Board debate. Now that they have been removed, they will be unable to do so.” I personally tried to speak by telephone with the Deputy Leader of the Opposition. When I could not reach him, I telephoned Ministers M.K. Stalin, Duraimurugan, Veerapandi Arumugam and K.N. Nehru and asked them to go to the Opposition Leader's room, conciliate the AIADMK members and invite them to take part in the electricity debate. I also spoke by telephone with the Speaker, asking him to withdraw the expulsion order and invite them in the House to return. He issued that invitation accordingly.

I have already quoted in this letter what our friend Tirunavukkarasu said about the events of 25 March 1989. What happened when, as Finance Minister, I began to present the Budget? Jayalalithaa, then Leader of the Opposition, rose and said, “The Chief Minister is a criminal offender; he must not read the Budget,” and tried to snatch the Budget from my hand. AIADMK members surrounded her in support and shouted, “Do not read it! Do not read it!” The member for Anthiyur grabbed the Budget book from my hand, pulled it away and tore it up. When I tried to stop him, he struck me in the face and my spectacles fell and shattered. Minister Veerapandi Arumugam was struck on the head with a microphone stand and taken to hospital bleeding.

Do you know what punishment was imposed then even for an incident as grave and brutal as this? A resolution was passed temporarily suspending the AIADMK members who caused the disturbance for one week. But on 27 March 1989, when literary figure Kumari Ananthan, then Deputy Leader of the Congress Legislature Party, asked for even that temporary suspension to be reconsidered, I said:

“Neither I nor this Government has the narrow-minded desire to conduct the proceedings of this House without the opposition parties. Let what has happened remain in the past; let what happens hereafter be good. I approach this issue in that spirit. I request that the resolution passed that day in the reflected heat of an emotional outburst be withdrawn and that the Leader of the Opposition and the others return to work in the House.”
That is the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam. Even Bharat Ratna C. Subramaniam praised this through the newspapers at the time.

When the DMK was in office, AIADMK leader Jayalalithaa did not attend Assembly sessions and did not even sign the register. A member loses the seat after being absent from the Assembly beyond a prescribed number of days. When this same Panneerselvam moved a resolution on the AIADMK's behalf so that Jayalalithaa could continue as an MLA, the DMK could have refused to vote for it. Everyone knows, however, that the DMK displayed its characteristic magnanimity, accepted her absence from the House and helped the resolution pass unanimously.

Why, after M.G.R., the AIADMK's founder, split from the DMK, how many days did he stay away from the Assembly? How many sessions did Jayalalithaa miss? Even now, I have already explained twice why I do not attend the House. I have said that I am ready to attend if seating suited to my physical condition is provided. Does an AIADMK Government that refuses to arrange it possess any moral right to ask why I do not attend?

Finally, Panneerselvam says that if Karunanidhi has the courage to come and speak in the Assembly, he should come! Seeing that your name was Panneerselvam, I once held a good opinion of you. Only now do I understand that it is like naming a dark-complexioned man “Vellaiyan,” the fair one.

You ask whether I have the courage to come and speak in the Assembly! I stood and argued in the very House once dominated by stalwarts such as Perunthalaivar Kamaraj, the elder statesman Bhaktavatsalam, C. Subramaniam, R. Venkataraman, G.K. Moopanar, K. Vinayagam, Ponnappa Nadar, M. Kalyanasundaram, P. Ramamurthi, M.R. Venkataraman, Umanath, K.T.K. Thangamani, Ananthanayaki, Kumari Ananthan and Manali Kandasamy. Do not forget that after hearing my argument, even my dear friend M.G.R. left saying that he would return and answer the next day!

If you have courage, arrange a place in the Assembly where I can sit comfortably and send word to me! Do not grow arrogant because a gust of wind has given a leaf the chance to cling to the summit of a tower! If the wind blows a little harder, do you know where that leaf will fall? Whether a Chief Minister is a “benami” or a “proxy,” occupying that office requires composure and civility. Learn that! Try to acquire them!
With affection,
M.K.
28 November 2014
